steps.title: Pin down each author's stance
steps.content: Text 1 calls the shared EGR gene an "especially promising discovery" and frames it as "exciting progress" toward understanding human regeneration. Text 2 reports the same finding but warns that the gene "likely functions very differently in humans than it does in panther worms" and that how it operates in humans "remains unclear."

steps.title: Predict Text 2's verdict on Text 1
steps.content: Because Text 2 stresses unresolved differences, the Text 2 author would view Text 1's enthusiastic framing as getting ahead of the evidence — in other words, too optimistic.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It is reasonable given that Srivastava and her team have identified how EGR functions in both humans and panther worms.’: Text 2 explicitly says the human mechanism is unclear, so this overreaches what Text 2 reports. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘It is overly optimistic given additional observations from Srivastava and her team.’: matches Text 2's caution that the gene works differently and its human role is unknown. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘It is unexpected given that Srivastava and her team's findings were generally met with enthusiasm.’: Text 2 itself notes the finding "caused excitement," so optimism is not unexpected — misreads Text 1's tone. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘It is unfairly dismissive given the progress that Srivastava and her team have reported.’: Text 1 is enthusiastic, not dismissive — opposite of what the passage says. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
